We can rewrite the congruence as:
\[ 3(6x + 1) \equiv 4 \pmod{p} \]
Dividing both sides by 3 (assuming \( p \neq 3 \)), we get:
\[ 6x + 1 \equiv \frac{4}{3} \pmod{p} \]

This congruence will have no solution in \( x \) if 2 does not have an inverse modulo \( p \). This happens when \( \gcd(2, p) \neq 1 \). Since \( p \) is a prime number, \( \gcd(2, p) \neq 1 \) only if \( p = 2 \).

However, we also need to check the case when \( p = 3 \):
\[ 3(6x + 1) \equiv 4 \pmod{3} \]
\[ 0 \not\equiv 1 \pmod{3} \]
So when \( p = 3 \), there is no solution in \( x \).

Thus, the only prime numbers \( p \) for which there is no solution in \( x \) are \( p = 2 \) and \( p = 3 \). The sum of these prime numbers is \( 2 + 3 = 5 \).

\[ 3(6x + 1) \equiv 4 \pmod{3} \]
\[ 0 \not\equiv 1 \pmod{3} \]
